paper_title: Experimental Evidence on the Productivity Effects of Generative Artificial Intelligence

issues_found.what_extractor_says: evidence_type='longitudinal_field' for the two-week followup ChatGPT use rates
issues_found.what_paper_says: The two-week followup is itself a survey conducted within the same RCT sample (treatment vs. control), so the comparison of 33% vs 18% is still an RCT-derived contrast (downstream behavioral outcome), not a free-standing longitudinal_field study. evidence_type 'rct' would more accurately reflect the design basis. This is a minor, possibly defensible classification choice (the outcome is measured longitudinally), so severity is low.
issues_found.evidence_quote: Page 11: 'To track whether participants are subsequently using ChatGPT in their real jobs, we resurvey them two weeks after their completion of the initial survey... 33% of former treatment group participants have used ChatGPT in their job in the past week, relative to 18% of control group participants.'

issues_found.what_extractor_says: effect_size=0.5 for job satisfaction (with note that text reports ~0.40 SDs and Figure 4 reports 0.5)
issues_found.what_paper_says: Page 9 prose explicitly states 'ChatGPT substantially increases job satisfaction, by about 0.40 standard deviations (p = 0.000)'. The figure label on Figure 4 Panel (a) shows 'Treatment Effect: 0.5 SDs, 95% CI: [0.32, 0.68]'. The extractor flagged the discrepancy in an 'additional' note, which is appropriate transparency, but selected 0.5 as the headline effect_size while the in-text discussion uses 0.40. This is a borderline case—the extractor is honest about the inconsistency. Recommend flagging as a paper-internal inconsistency rather than misattribution; severity low.
issues_found.evidence_quote: Page 9: 'Figure 4 Panel (a) shows that ChatGPT substantially increases job satisfaction, by about 0.40 standard deviations (p = 0.000).' Figure 4(a) label: 'Treatment Effect: 0.5 SDs 95% CI: [0.32, 0.68]'.

spot_checks_passed.verified: Sample N=444 college-educated professionals across marketers, grant writers, consultants, data analysts, HR professionals, and managers, with 50% randomly assigned to ChatGPT treatment between tasks 1 and 2 — verbatim match to page 2 Introduction. Note: user's prompt mentioned N~453, but the paper consistently states 444; extraction correctly uses 444.

Time taken effect: 'time taken on the post-treatment task drops by 10 minutes (37%) relative to the control group, who take an average of 27 minutes (p = 0.000)' (page 3). Direction is negative (time goes DOWN), correctly captured as relation='negative'. Figure 1(a) confirms Treatment Effect: -0.83 SDs, 95% CI [-0.63, -1.03]; extraction's effect_size=-0.83 with CI [-1.03, -0.63] is correct.

Quality grade effect: 'Average evaluator grades in the treatment group increase by 0.45 standard deviations (p = 0.000)' (page 3). Figure 1(b) confirms 0.45 SDs, 95% CI [0.27, 0.63]. Direction positive (productivity UP). Extraction matches exactly. No flipping of directions.

Inequality moderator claim: control slope 0.491 (SE 0.053), treatment slope 0.248 (SE 0.065), Change in Slope -0.243 with 95% CI [-0.08, -0.41] and p=0.004 (Figure 2(a), page 6). Extraction correctly captures both slopes, change-in-slope, and p-value. The interpretation 'low-ability gains more, gap closes' is correctly captured by relation='positive' for baseline ability moderated by ChatGPT access.

Inequality reduction driven by lower-ability participants benefiting more: 'participants who scored lower on the first round benefit more from ChatGPT access, as the figure shows: the gap between the treatment and control lines is much larger at the left-hand end of the x-axis' (page 5). Direction (negative effect on inequality) correctly captured.

Fixed-time subarm: '20% of participants... required to spend exactly 15 minutes... treatment increases grades by a similar 0.39 standard deviations (p = 0.13), albeit imprecisely estimated' (page 4). Extraction matches; sample_size=89 is a reasonable estimate for 20% of 444.
Attrition 5% control / 10% treatment, balance differences on only employment status and HR-professional indicator out of 13 pre-treatment characteristics — verbatim match to page 2.

design_basis='rct' is correctly tagged. The paper is a preregistered RCT (AEARCTR-0010882, page 1 footnote): 'In a preregistered online experiment, we assign occupation-specific, incentivized writing tasks to 444 college-educated professionals, and randomly expose half of them to ChatGPT.' author_framing='causal' is also justified by language like 'This design allows us to estimate the causal effects of ChatGPT' (page 2).

Meta-claim accurately summarizes the abstract: time -0.8 SDs, quality +0.4 SDs, inequality reduction by benefiting low-ability workers more, substitution > complementarity, restructured tasks toward editing/away-from-rough-drafting, increased job satisfaction and self-efficacy, heightened both worry and excitement. Verbatim alignment with abstract on page 1. No direction flips.
